questions.question: A water utility has demand P = 100 - Q and cost C(Q) = 400 + 20Q. If it is unregulated and maximizes profit, what output and price does it choose, and what is its profit?
questions.explanation: MR = 100 - 2Q and MC = 20. Setting MR = MC gives Q = 40 and P = 60. Profit is 60(40) - 400 - 20(40) = 1,200. B gives marginal-cost pricing and a loss. C maximizes revenue. D gives monopoly quantity but marginal-cost price.

questions.question: A natural monopoly has demand P = 100 - Q and cost C(Q) = 900 + 10Q. What are the marginal-cost pricing output and profit?
questions.explanation: Marginal-cost pricing sets P = MC = 10. Demand gives 100 - Q = 10, so Q = 90. Revenue is 900 and cost is 900 + 900 = 1,800, so profit is -900. B is the unregulated monopoly result. C is not tied to MC or ATC pricing. D uses the monopoly price at the marginal-cost quantity.

questions.question: A natural monopoly has C(Q) = F + 10Q and demand P = 70 - Q. If fixed cost F rises from 400 to 900, what happens to marginal-cost pricing and average-cost pricing?

choices.A: Marginal-cost price and output stay at P = 10, Q = 60; average-cost pricing requires a higher price because ATC rises.
choices.B: Marginal-cost price rises because fixed cost is part of marginal cost.
choices.C: Average-cost price falls because higher fixed cost creates stronger economies of scale.
choices.D: Both marginal-cost and average-cost prices stay unchanged because demand is unchanged.

questions.explanation: Fixed cost does not affect MC, so marginal-cost pricing remains P = 10 and Q = 60. But ATC = F/Q + 10 rises when F rises, so break-even average-cost pricing requires a higher price. B confuses fixed and marginal cost. C reverses the ATC effect at any given Q. D ignores average cost.

questions.question: A natural monopoly initially has demand P = 80 - Q and cost C(Q) = 500 + 20Q. Demand expands to P = 100 - Q. What happens to the unregulated monopoly output?

choices.A: It rises from 30 to 40 because the MR curve shifts upward while MC is unchanged.
choices.B: It stays at 30 because cost conditions define natural monopoly output.
choices.C: It rises from 60 to 80 because monopoly sets P = MC.
choices.D: It falls from 40 to 30 because higher demand raises price.

questions.explanation: Initially MR = 80 - 2Q; setting MR = 20 gives Q = 30. After demand expands, MR = 100 - 2Q; setting MR = 20 gives Q = 40. B ignores demand. C gives competitive quantities. D reverses the output effect.

questions.question: In a natural monopoly graph, average cost declines over the entire relevant range and lies above marginal cost. Demand crosses MC at Q = 100, but at that quantity price is below average cost. What does this graph imply?

choices.A: Marginal-cost pricing is allocatively efficient but causes losses unless subsidized.
choices.B: Marginal-cost pricing gives positive profit because MC is below AC.
choices.C: Average-cost pricing is more efficient than marginal-cost pricing because it produces more output.
choices.D: The firm should shut down because declining average cost means marginal cost is negative.

questions.explanation: P = MC produces the efficient quantity, but if P < ATC the firm loses money. B reverses the implication of P below ATC. C is wrong because average-cost pricing generally reduces output relative to MC pricing. D incorrectly infers negative MC from declining AC.

questions.question: A regulator considers average-cost pricing for a natural gas utility. On the graph, demand intersects ATC at Q = 70 and P = 45, while demand intersects MC at Q = 100 and P = 20. Which interpretation is correct?

choices.A: Average-cost pricing breaks even at Q = 70 but leaves some efficient units from 70 to 100 unserved.
choices.B: Average-cost pricing produces Q = 100 and requires a subsidy.
choices.C: Marginal-cost pricing breaks even because P = MC.
choices.D: Unregulated monopoly must produce Q = 100 because demand intersects MC there.

questions.explanation: Average-cost pricing sets P = ATC where demand crosses ATC, allowing zero economic profit but less than efficient output. B describes marginal-cost pricing with possible subsidy. C confuses covering marginal cost with covering average cost. D applies the competitive condition to monopoly.

questions.question: Why can one firm serve a natural monopoly market at lower total cost than two or more firms?

choices.A: Large fixed costs and economies of scale make average cost fall as output expands, so splitting output duplicates fixed costs.
choices.B: Natural monopolies always have zero fixed cost and constant average cost.
choices.C: Natural monopolies exist only because governments ban entry.
choices.D: A natural monopoly must have marginal cost above average cost at all outputs.

questions.explanation: Natural monopoly arises when scale economies are strong enough that one firm can produce the market output at lower cost than multiple firms. B contradicts the usual fixed-cost logic. C describes legal monopoly, not necessarily natural monopoly. D would imply rising average cost, not natural monopoly.

questions.question: A town is deciding whether to allow multiple firms to build competing sewer networks. Each network requires a $5 million pipe system before serving any household, and marginal service cost is low. Which model applies and what is the main regulatory dilemma?

choices.A: Natural monopoly; marginal-cost pricing is efficient but may not cover average cost.
choices.B: Perfect competition; many firms should enter until price equals average fixed cost.
choices.C: Third-degree price discrimination; each firm should charge different neighborhoods different prices.
choices.D: Monopolistic competition; product differentiation eliminates the fixed-cost problem.

questions.explanation: Large fixed infrastructure and low marginal cost create natural monopoly conditions. The dilemma is that P = MC can generate losses, while P = ATC breaks even but restricts output. B ignores duplicated fixed costs. C may be a pricing strategy but not the core market structure. D misclassifies the infrastructure problem.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a natural monopoly from a legal monopoly?

choices.A: A natural monopoly comes from cost conditions such as declining average cost; a legal monopoly comes from legal barriers such as patents or exclusive licenses.
choices.B: A natural monopoly is always created by patents; a legal monopoly is always created by economies of scale.
choices.C: A natural monopoly has many firms, while a legal monopoly has one firm.
choices.D: A natural monopoly sets P = MC, while a legal monopoly sets MR = MC.

questions.explanation: Natural monopoly is based on technology and cost structure. Legal monopoly is based on law or regulation restricting entry. B reverses the definitions. C is false because both can involve one firm. D confuses market structure with regulatory pricing.

questions.question: A natural monopoly has demand P = 100 - Q and cost C(Q) = 1,600 + 20Q. Under unregulated monopoly, what is the deadweight loss relative to marginal-cost pricing?
questions.explanation: MR = 100 - 2Q. Monopoly output solves MR = 20, so Qm = 40 and Pm = 60. Marginal-cost pricing output solves P = 20, so Qc = 80. DWL = 0.5(80 - 40)(60 - 20) = 800. Fixed cost affects profit but not this marginal welfare triangle. B and C use incorrect triangle dimensions. D treats the welfare loss as a larger rectangle.
